title: Write a Post-Interview Thank-You Message
summary: Send a brief, polite thank-you message after a job interview to show appreciation and reinforce your interest.
learnerPromise: You will be able to write a short, professional thank-you message within 24 hours of an interview that leaves a positive impression.
whyItMatters: A thank-you message shows good manners and keeps you fresh in the interviewer's mind. Many candidates skip this step, so doing it can help you stand out.
realLifeUses: Emailing the person who interviewed you the evening after your meeting
Sending a brief thank-you text if you were interviewed by a small business owner who prefers texting
Writing a thank-you note to a teacher or mentor who connected you with a job opportunity
Following up after a casual informational conversation about possible work
materialsNeeded: Email address or phone number of the interviewer
Notes from the interview to reference
Phone or computer to send the message
result: A sent thank-you email or message that is polite, brief, and specific to the conversation.
youWillLearn: How to write a subject line that is clear and easy to find
How to thank the interviewer specifically for something they shared or explained
How to restate your interest in the role in one sentence
How to keep the message short and send it within 24 hours
miniChallenge: Write a thank-you email for an imaginary interview. Mention something specific from the conversation, restate your interest, and keep it under five sentences.
steps: Within 24 hours of the interview, open your email or messaging app and start a new message.
Use a clear subject line like 'Thank you — [Your Name]' or 'Thank you for the interview today.'
Start by thanking the interviewer by name for their time and for telling you about the role or company.
Mention one specific thing from the conversation that stood out to you, such as a detail about the team or the work.
Close by saying you remain interested in the position and look forward to hearing from them. Sign with your full name and phone number.
completionCriteria: Your message is sent within 24 hours of the interview
The message includes a specific detail from your conversation and a clear statement of your interest
commonProblems: Forgetting to send it — set a reminder on your phone for a few hours after the interview
Writing too much — keep it to three to five sentences; the interviewer is busy
Being too generic — mentioning a specific detail shows you were paying attention and care
Sending it to the wrong email address — double-check the spelling before sending
tips: If you interviewed with multiple people, send a separate message to each person, slightly personalized
If you do not have the interviewer's email, ask the receptionist or send it through the person who scheduled the interview
Avoid asking about pay or benefits in the thank-you message — this is for appreciation, not negotiation
If you forgot to mention something important during the interview, you can include one brief sentence about it
